retrieved_docs.title: Temporary Abdominal Closure Techniques -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Trauma and hemorrhage

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Junctional Hemorrhage Control -- Technique or Treatment -- Hemostatic Dressings
retrieved_docs.content: Factor concentrators: rapidly absorb water, increasing the concentration of clotting factors and platelets at the injury site Mucoadhesive agents: strongly adhere to injured tissue and physically seal the bleeding wound Procoagulants: supplement hemostatic factors Evidence does not support one type of hemostatic dressing over the others. [16] However, the following hemostatic dressings are recommended by the CoTCCC:

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhage Control -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: One product, made of smectite minerals, was developed to both concentrate clotting elements by desiccation and activate the intrinsic coagulation pathway. While the products made of smectite minerals proved to be highly effective at hemostasis, studies showed that it caused thrombi to form that could migrate throughout the circulatory system, resulting in devastating complications. A surgical gauze impregnated with kaolin and aluminum silicate also proved to be effective at activating the intrinsic pathway and facilitating hemostasis without known side effects. Following these studies, the CoTCCC decided in 2015 to recommend surgical gauze impregnated with kaolin and aluminum silicate as the "hemostatic agent of choice." [12] Two chitosan-based dressings were recommended as alternatives to the surgical gauze impregnated with kaolin and aluminum silicate. Lastly, a syringe that injects expandable sponges into the wound tract to create a physical barrier and pressure inside the cavity proved highly effective against narrow but deep penetrating wounds in some regions.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Junctional Hemorrhage Control -- Technique or Treatment -- Hemostatic Dressings
retrieved_docs.content: XStat: A pre-loaded syringe-like applicator filled with small, chitosan-coated cellulose sponges. XStat is a mucoadhesive—it seals the wound and expands up to 12 times its volume, thus promoting internal compression and hemostasis. [11] The coated mini-sponges are not biodegradable and contain radiopaque markers that guide removal later. XStat can control hemorrhage in deep-tract and narrow-entrance wounds occurring in penetrating trauma, as in gunshot junctional area wounds. Research reveals that XStat can adequately control hemorrhage not amenable to tourniquet placement or other means, even in junctional injury cases. [23] However, XStat is more difficult and time-intensive to remove than simple gauze packing. The agent is contraindicated in severe intra-thoracic, intra-pelvic, and intra-abdominal injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Penetrating Abdominal Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Liver injury can be mild, requiring observation and serial exams, or can be acutely lethal. In diffuse hepatic bleeding, wrapping and packing the liver allows for the correction of coagulopathy and is typically left for approximately 48 hours. Earlier reexploration is associated with a greater rebleeding risk. Rarely an emergency hepatectomy is performed, but it is associated with a very high mortality. Results from a single-center study examining nonoperative management of hepatic trauma concluded that approximately 20% of penetrating hepatic trauma could be managed nonoperatively. Different techniques such as packing or suturing, or omental patching as initial operative measures were suggested, in combination with embolization as needed for arterial injury, instead of large resections when possible for both blunt and penetrating trauma. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Post-procedural Bleeding
retrieved_docs.content: Adhesive agents: cyanoacrylate glue, fibrin glue, and chlorhexidine bio-adhesive gel Absorbable agents: oxidized cellulose, gel foam, collagen fleeces or sponges, and gelatin sponge Biologic agents: topical thrombin, green tea extract, Haemocoagulase, and Ankaferd Blood Stopper Sealant agents: acrylic or surgical splints and calcium alginate Pharmacotherapies: local antifibrinolytic solutions (ie, tranexamic acid mouthwash) and gauze soaked in the tranexamic acid

retrieved_docs.title: Splenic Rupture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical management is indicated in cases of persistent hemodynamic instability, signs of peritonitis, expanding intra-abdominal hematoma, or failure of nonoperative treatment. Exploratory laparotomy remains the standard surgical approach. When appropriate, splenic preservation techniques such as splenorrhaphy, partial splenectomy, or topical hemostatic agents may be employed. In cases involving extensive parenchymal disruption or uncontrolled hemorrhage, splenectomy is frequently required and may be life-saving.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporary Abdominal Closure Techniques -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In instances of severe trauma, TAC may be employed as part of damage control surgery to control bleeding and contamination.

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management -- Hemostatic Powders and Gels
retrieved_docs.content: Hemostatic gel matrix is a viscous, transparent peptide that self-assembles after contact with tissue or blood, creating a sealant. Use in GI bleeding comes primarily from endoscopic submucosal dissection data showing the reduced need for coagulation forceps to stop oozing postendoscopic submucosal dissection when the gel is applied to the defect base. [64] A prior observational pilot study of 111 patients presenting with GI bleeding, the majority peptic ulcer or post-polypectomy bleeds, showed 94% hemostasis when used as a primary agent. Spurting bleeding was seen in only 6% of patients, and rebleeding was noted in 16% of cases within 30 days. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Duodenal Perforation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of duodenal perforations depends on the type of perforation. Two kinds of perforation are defined as contained or non-contained perforation. Non-contained perforation is divided into 2 subgroups: minor or major.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Perforation and Tears -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical intervention is often necessary, particularly in cases with extensive contamination, significant tissue necrosis, or delayed diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporary Abdominal Closure Techniques -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding Hemorrhage can occur during or after the TAC procedure. Adequate hemostasis during the initial surgery and vigilant postoperative monitoring are crucial to prevent and address bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management -- Hemostatic Powders and Gels
retrieved_docs.content: However, hemostatic powder shows promise as a primary treatment for malignant bleeding. A multicenter RCT of 106 patients randomized to hemostatic powder or standard therapies for active tumor bleeding involving the gastrointestinal tract showed increased initial hemostasis and low 30-day rebleeding in the group receiving hemostatic powder. No difference was seen in 6-month mortality. A potential explanation for these results may be reduced mechanical disruption (eg, coagulation, injection, clip) to the friable tumor tissue with hemostatic powder. [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Epistaxis Nasal Pack -- Technique or Treatment -- Patients With Underlying Coagulopathy
retrieved_docs.content: In the emergency department, additional attempts to cauterize bleeding or repacking the naris with the addition of tranexamic acid typically results in successful hemostasis. [15] Some clinicians avoid using packs requiring removal in coagulopathic patients and opt for materials that absorb over time, such as cellulose mesh, hemostatic matrix, or sinusotomy absorbable nasal packing. These materials may not provide pressure as effectively as inflatable tampons or balloons. However, they are less likely to injure the mucosa during placement and cannot damage the mucosa on removal because removal is not required.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic hemostasis is the first-line treatment for patients with bleeding esophageal ulcers. Standard-of-care therapies include injection, mechanical clips, and thermal coagulation. Newer interventions for hemostasis include topical hemostatic powders and gels. Refractory bleeding may warrant interventional radiology and surgery consultations. The treatment of esophageal ulcers depends on the underlying etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Appendectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Assess the staple lines to confirm hemostasis and integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy and تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) are both classified under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد (obstetric therapeutic procedures). By recognizing these interventions as subcomponents of the broader category of obstetric therapeutic care, clinicians can better organize treatment options, understand their related indications, and apply appropriate management strategies within the field of obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2769
retrieved_docs.content: Low transverse cesarean section is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), indicating that this surgical approach to delivery may be linked to an increased occurrence of early membrane rupture, a factor that clinicians must consider when planning obstetric care and managing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Repair of current obstetric laceration is a specific type of repair of obstetric laceration, representing a more focused procedure within the broader category of obstetric laceration repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 163
retrieved_docs.content: is inversely related to “repair of current obstetric laceration,” reflecting a reciprocal classification within the repair hierarchy. Collectively, these entities illustrate a comprehensive hierarchical framework in which antenatal/postnatal care, delivery findings, surgical manipulation techniques, obstetric manipulations, and repair procedures are interrelated, guiding clinical decision‑making and procedural selection across diverse birth scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2383
retrieved_docs.content: OBSTETRICS: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS is classified as a subcategory of the broader clinical activity of repair of current obstetric laceration, indicating that all surgical interventions aimed at repairing, closing, or reconstructing tissues injured during childbirth are encompassed within the general task of addressing obstetric lacerations. This hierarchical relationship underscores that procedures grouped under OBSTETRICS: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS are specifically intended to manage and heal the immediate tissue damage that occurs in the obstetric setting, thereby guiding clinicians toward appropriate surgical management of current obstetric injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean section is clinically associated with the low transverse cesarean section technique, and the low transverse cesarean section is in turn clinically associated with early membrane rupture (تمزق الأغشية الباكر). This relationship highlights that the specific low transverse incision used during a cesarean delivery may be linked to an increased likelihood of premature rupture of the fetal membranes, a factor that clinicians consider when planning surgical approaches and managing obstetric risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 137
retrieved_docs.content: delivery by vacuum extraction, and delivery of a dead fetus. Repair of episiotomy and other obstetric repairs are placed under OBSTETRICS: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS, a subcategory of obstetric surgical procedures. Overall, the interconnected hierarchy links routine obstetric care, assisted‑delivery techniques, surgical repairs, and life‑threatening complications, providing a comprehensive framework for precise clinical decision‑making and patient management throughout the full scope of childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of cerclage from cervix is a medical intervention that is directly related to the cervical cerclage procedure (إجراء تطويق عنق الرحم). This step involves extracting the sutures that were placed around the cervix to reinforce cervical competence and prevent premature dilation, and it represents a specific phase within the overall management of cervical insufficiency. Recognizing the connection between the removal of the cerclage and the broader cerclage procedure is essential for appropriate patient care and timing of intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery is a broad obstetric category that encompasses the specific outcome of فشل التوليد بالملقط (forceps delivery failure). Within this framework, الولادة بالملقط (forceps delivery) is directly linked to فشل التوليد بالملقط, indicating that the failure represents a particular adverse event or subcategory of the forceps delivery procedure. Consequently, both الولادة بالملقط and its associated failure, فشل التوليد بالملقط, are situated under the larger grouping of other procedures that induce or assist delivery, highlighting their interrelated roles in assisted vaginal birth and the importance of recognizing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 734
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad category of maternal complications that encompasses several specific conditions. It has as child entities postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض), and fetal death (موت الجنين). In addition, this category is classified as obstetric cervical tear (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic obstetric hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). Together, these interrelated complications illustrate the diverse and serious events that can arise during the postpartum period and influence the clinical management of the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery” also includes the concrete example فشل التوليد بالملقط (failed delivery with forceps). Collectively, this hierarchical and functional network links broad procedural categories (ante‑partum, intra‑partum, surgical manipulation, cervical incision, induction, pregnancy‑related care) to their specific clinical actions (amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling, fetal reduction, various breech and forceps deliveries, obstetrical version techniques, membrane rupture, cervical dilation, trachelotomy, evacuation of retained products, ectopic‑pregnancy surgery, episiotomy repair). Understanding these interrelationships supports clinicians in selecting appropriate obstetric interventions, clarifying synonymous terminology across languages, and optimizing patient care throughout pregnancy management and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 852
retrieved_docs.content: of obstetric procedures—ranging from placental operations to laceration repairs—highlighting their anatomical focus on the uterus, their hierarchical classification, and the clinical sequelae such as uterine scarring that may follow uterine interventions. This integrated understanding aids clinicians in categorizing procedures, anticipating site‑specific outcomes, and managing postoperative uterine conditions.
